titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 21 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). Spender discusses the business of his daily routine, the therapeutic power of nature, and the tensions between himself and the Central Unionist Office. He also mentions financial matters.Sir Wilfrid Spender was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War. Lady Lillian served in the Ulster Volunteer Nursing Corps during the war, and was an organiser of the Ulster Division Comforts Fund.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
21 June My p r Not a very long letter from you but
I am glad to see you were enjoying your visit
at the Powells. I hope he seems more content
to look on & though he is so fit it may be
best. There is a moment's pause at
present but still plenty to be done, tho' I
try to make a point of 7 hours in bed
when I can get it. Yesterday evening I went out for a
walk to exercise & found a little wood
with big oxeyed daisies something closely
resembling an anchusa with its beautiful
blue & other wild flowers such as one has
not seen for many a day, & one forgot
about the war in the delights of one's
flower visions â undisturbed by any thoughts of course
of the rodent animals which prey upon them â
Even a rose is safe with a wolf - when
he is alone far different from what would
happen if a precious pr were there. I sent Douglas up to my post
yesterday and he seemed quite well
pleased with it & thought it quite nice. The We are laying in a water supply
and provisions so that we can stop them
if we want to. We had some French officers to dinner
